selection.greatFrontendReasonIfOmitted: GreatFrontEnd does not appear to have a directly relevant quiz or guide focused on why `eval()` is dangerous.

competitors.label: MDN eval
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the canonical competitor for `eval()`, especially around security, scope, and safer replacements.
competitors.winReason: MDN remains the broader reference, but our page now wins this interview-sized comparison because it adds a sharper same-origin attack path and explicit before/after replacements for bracket access and JSON.parse.
theirStrengths: It covers arbitrary code execution, CSP restrictions, and safer alternatives in detail.
theirGaps: It is more reference-style and less aggressively framed around interview-grade risk language.

realWorldUseCases: eval() turns data into executable code. In production that creates injection risk, CSP friction, and runtime behavior that is much harder to debug or optimize. In the browser, a query param, CMS field, or localStorage value can become same-origin script with DOM access and user-session privileges.
MDN already explains code-execution risk, but our page is now sharper on the browser-origin attack path from untrusted data to executable code.
actionableExamples: MDN has solid safer-replacement guidance, while our page now adds one compact before/after example for both bracket access and JSON.parse.
followUpConfusion: MDN is still broader on related reference details, so the advanced follow-up set remains close.
Add one short note on eval-like alternatives such as the Function constructor if we want a broader advanced follow-up set.

competitors.label: DigitalOcean eval tutorial
competitors.selectionReason: This tutorial is a common explanatory competitor for `eval()` risks and trade-offs.
competitors.winReason: Our page is tighter on same-origin attack surface, CSP, and runtime predictability, while DigitalOcean stays more general.
theirStrengths: It calls out malicious input, performance cost, and the small class of metaprogramming cases where `eval` still appears.
theirGaps: It is weaker on CSP friction and on the debugging/optimizer cost as a frontend mental model.

realWorldUseCases: eval() turns data into executable code. In production that creates injection risk, CSP friction, and runtime behavior that is much harder to debug or optimize. In the browser, a query param, CMS field, or localStorage value can become same-origin script with DOM access and user-session privileges.
followUpConfusion: JavaScript engines optimize code when structure is predictable. eval adds dynamic unknown code paths, which can force conservative assumptions and reduce optimization opportunities in surrounding scope.

realWorldUseCases: DigitalOcean covers malicious input and performance cost, but our page now connects that risk more directly to same-origin browser privileges.
actionableExamples: Its examples of risky eval usage are concrete enough that the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: Our page does a better job connecting the security answer to CSP and engine-optimization side effects.

actionableExamples: Add one explicit function-dispatch replacement for user-selected formulas if we want a larger example edge over general tutorials.

competitors.label: Stack Overflow eval bad idea
competitors.selectionReason: This thread is a direct community-Q&A competitor for the exact search intent behind the question.
competitors.winReason: The Stack Overflow thread covers the classic warnings, but our page now wins this comparison because it turns them into a sharper browser-security mental model with cleaner grouped replacements.
theirStrengths: It covers the two classic concerns clearly: code injection and performance.
theirGaps: It is an answer thread, so the reasoning is less consolidated than a purpose-built editorial page.

realWorldUseCases: A rules engine receives a string from an admin panel and runs it with eval. A malformed or malicious rule can crash checkout logic or expose sensitive browser data because the payload now executes as code inside your origin, not as inert data.
followUpConfusion: JavaScript engines optimize code when structure is predictable. eval adds dynamic unknown code paths, which can force conservative assumptions and reduce optimization opportunities in surrounding scope.

realWorldUseCases: The Stack Overflow thread clearly mentions code injection and performance, but our page now describes the browser-origin consequence more concretely.
actionableExamples: Its examples are practical enough that the example criterion remains close.
followUpConfusion: Our page is clearer on the next question of why eval affects both security policy and engine optimization.

actionableExamples: Add one tiny before/after example for allowlisted expression parsing if we want a stronger alternative-pattern edge over community threads.
